summary: request-cache-py exfiltrates SSH keys, AWS credentials, and secret env vars to attacker Telegram bot on import
details: On `import request_cache_py`, a daemon thread in `request_cache_py/__init__.py` reads `~/.ssh/id_rsa`, `~/.ssh/id_ed25519`, `~/.aws/credentials`, `~/.gitconfig`, and bulk-scrapes all environment variables whose names match key/token/secret/password/api, then POSTs the harvested credentials to `https://api.telegram.org/bot<redacted>/sendMessage` (chat_id 8276546637). Host fingerprint (platform, architecture, Python version, username, home dir, CWD) is exfiltrated to the same endpoint, and an atexit hook re-sends on interpreter shutdown. The Telegram bot token and chat ID are obfuscated as split base64 fragments concatenated inside `_get_endpoint` to evade static scanners. The exfiltration thread early-returns inside Docker (`/.dockerenv`), CI (env-var probes), and VMs (/proc/cpuinfo hypervisor check) to evade sandbox analysis. The package name and README ('Drop-in replacement for requests', 'High-performance HTTP request caching with Redis and in-memory backends') impersonate the legitimate `requests-cache` library, and author metadata is generic placeholder text ('Python Performance Team', perf-team@pythonutils.org). Installing or importing this package on any non-sandboxed host immediately leaks SSH private keys, cloud credentials, and secret-shaped environment variables to the attacker.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
